raw_case:
Intraperitoneal hemorrhage due to accessory spleen torsion: Case report
Torsion of an accessory spleen is a rare condition, and it is often difficult to diagnose. It frequently presents as an acute abdomen, especially in young individuals. Only several dozen cases have been reported so far, and instances of rupture are even rarer. In this report, we present a case of an adult male who developed intraperitoneal hemorrhage due to torsion of an accessory spleen, presenting with left upper quadrant pain. A preoperative diagnosis was successfully made using contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT), Angiography and magnetic resonance imaging(MRI). When encountering left upper abdominal pain or intraperitoneal hemorrhage, torsion of an accessory spleen should be considered as a differential diagnosis.

Case report

A man in his 20s presented to the emergency department with acute left upper abdominal pain. The patient had been experiencing a persistent feeling of tightness in the left upper abdomen since the evening of the previous day. By late at night, the pain became severe. The pain worsened with deep inhalation, when in the supine position. The patient had no history of trauma or injury.On physical exam, mild tachycardia was noted. Heart rate: 93 bpm, with no other abnormalities in vital signs. There was left upper quadrant pain, but tenderness was unclear. There was no abdominal distention, and no masses were palpated.Blood chemistry findings showed a mild elevation in WBC at 9,800/µl and CRP at 2.11 mg/dl, but all other values were within the normal range. Hemoglobin was 16.2 g/dl, indicating no anemia, and there were no coagulation abnormalities.Non-contrast CT revealed high attenuation around the spleen (Figs. 1A and B), suggestive of a hematoma. Contrast-enhanced CT showed no irregularities, stenosis, or dilation in the arteries, and there was no evidence of extravasation of contrast medium. The spleen displayed homogeneous enhancement, with no findings suggestive of splenic injury or spontaneous splenic rupture. A 25-mm round nodule was observed anterior to the spleen. The nodule had the same CT density as the hematoma and lacked contrast enhancement (Figs. 1C and D). Furthermore, it was broadly attached to the hematoma, so it was difficult to distinguish between them. However, it was slightly hypodense compared to the hematoma, and a cord-like structure extending from the splenic hilum to the nodule was seen, suggesting an accessory spleen. The absence of enhancement in the suspected accessory spleen and the presence of the surrounding hematoma suggested the possibility of accessory spleen rupture due to torsion.Angiography revealed occlusion and contrast retention near the splenic hilum on digital subtraction angiography (Fig. 2A). CT during Splenic Arteriography showed a tapered occlusion of the artery leading to the suspected accessory spleen (Figs. 2B–D). No contrast enhancement was observed in the nodule, and there was no evidence of extravasation. It was determined that the nodule was an accessory spleen and that it was not enhanced due to pedicle torsion. The cause of the hemorrhage was suspected to be vascular injury at the pedicle or rupture of the accessory spleen due to the torsion. Other possible causes, such as vasculitis and segmental arterial mediolysis (SAM), were considered, and angiography of the celiac artery, superior mesenteric artery, and both renal arteries was performed, but no abnormalities were found.On contrast-enhanced MRI performed several hours after angiography, the hematoma appeared slightly hyperintense compared to the spleen on pre-contrast T1-weighted images (Fig. 3A). In the arterial phase, no enhancement was observed in the nodule, but faint enhancement was seen at 120 seconds, and distinct delayed enhancement was noted at 10 minutes (Fig. 3B–D). This was considered to be gradual enhancement of the accessory spleen due to venous occlusion caused by pedicle torsion. On fat-suppressed T2-weighted images and diffusion-weighted images (DWI), the accessory spleen showed mildly hyperintense signals similar to the spleen, while the hematoma showed hypointense, and the border between the accessory spleen and the hematoma was clearly defined (Figs. 3E and F).Surgical and pathological findings: Intraoperatively, no evidence of pedicle torsion was observed, but the accessory spleen appeared slightly paler than normal. Pathological examination revealed hemorrhage and congestion within the accessory spleen, as well as bleeding from the pedicle. Based on these findings, the diagnosis was intraperitoneal hemorrhage due to torsion of the accessory spleen. It was concluded that the torsion caused bleeding from the pedicle, which then spontaneously resolved.

Discussion

Accessory spleens are small splenic tissues separate from the main spleen and are commonly found as incidental anatomical anomalies. The presence of accessory spleens is observed in approximately 10%-30% of the population [1].They have their own blood supply which is usually from a branch of the splenic artery [2]. Most accessory spleens are located in the left upper quadrant near the spleen. They may also be seen in the gastrocolic ligament, mesocolon, pancreas [3] or rarely the mediastinum [4,5], pelvis [6], scrotum [7].If the vascular pedicle is long, accessory spleens may be located anywhere in the intraperitoneal cavity, and there are cases of a “wandering accessory spleen” [8].Accessory spleens can undergo torsion, which may lead to abdominal pain or hemorrhage; however, this is a rare occurrence. A search on PubMed using the keywords ``accessory spleen'' and ``torsion'' reveals that there have been only about 70 reported cases from 1959 to 2024. There have been a few dozen reports of accessory spleen rupture, but these cases involve either spontaneous rupture of an enlarged accessory spleen due to hematologic disorders or rupture caused by trauma.There are 2 mechanisms for spontaneous rupture of an accessory spleen. One is when the capsule of an enlarged accessory spleen ruptures. The other is when blood flow disturbances in the pedicle, especially venous occlusion, which leads to bleeding from the pedicle [[9], [10], [11]]. In this case, the cause of the bleeding was pedicle injury from torsion.Torsion of the accessory spleen is more common when the accessory spleen is large, typically due to hematologic disorders or following splenectomy. Similarly, spontaneous rupture of an accessory spleen also tends to occur when the spleen is large. Torsion and hemorrhage of a small accessory spleen, such as one around 2 cm in size, like in this case, are rare [[12], [13], [14]].Accessory spleen torsion is a rare condition and is not widely recognized. There are no characteristic imaging findings, so it is difficult to diagnose before surgery. But, in recent years, there have been more reports, and with more awareness of the condition, the number of cases diagnosed and treated surgically may also be increasing. It is presumed that diagnosis is difficult when the accessory spleen is small, but when encountering acute abdomen, especially left upper abdominal pain in young individuals, it is important to consider the possibility of accessory spleen torsion. Additionally, several reports have noted that pain changes depending on body position in cases of accessory spleen torsion [[15], [16], [17]]. This may be due to changes in the degree of torsion or pressure on the accessory spleen. Although many reports suggest that pain worsens in standing or bending positions, in this case, the pain clearly worsened in the supine position. A clear change in pain with body position could be a helpful clue for diagnosis.In this case, as an accessory spleen was broadly attached to the hematoma and showed no contrast enhancement, the border between the 2 was unclear, making it difficult to identify the nodule on the CT scan. Given that the patient was a young individual with intraperitoneal hemorrhage, without any signs of inflammation or history of trauma, we considered conditions such as arterial rupture from segmental arterial mediolysis (SAM) or spontaneous splenic rupture. By carefully interpreting the arteries surrounding the hematoma, we noticed an occluded artery that extended to a non-enhancing nodule, which led us to suspect rupture caused by torsion of the accessory spleen. On CT during Splenic Angiography, the artery leading to the accessory spleen was clearly visualized. MRI revealed differences in signal intensity between the nodule and the hematoma, making it easier to identify the nodule and aiding in the diagnosis. The nodule and spleen showed similar signal intensities on T1-weighted, T2-weighted, and diffusion-weighted images, and the nodule was located near the splenic hilum, leading to a diagnosis of the nodule as an accessory spleen. While initial contrast-enhanced MRI showed no enhancement, delayed-phase imaging revealed slow enhancement, suggesting significant congestion. Given that the contrast enhancement progressed very slowly and the artery could be traced to the center of the accessory spleen on CT angiography, it was assumed that the torsion caused venous occlusion without arterial occlusion, meaning the torsion was "sub-torsed". Depending on the degree of infarction or congestion, US and MRI findings may vary [18]. However, in this case, the accessory spleen and the spleen showed similar signals on both US and MRI. Pathological findings confirmed that the accessory spleen was not infarcted, likely explaining the minimal signal changes, and congestion was mild. Furthermore, bleeding from the pedicle was confirmed on pathology. Based on both imaging and pathology, it was concluded that the accessory spleen had undergone torsion and hemorrhage, but the torsion was relieved early enough to prevent infarction or congestion.

Conclusion

An accessory spleen is a commonly observed anomaly, but torsion of the accessory spleen is a rare condition. Torsion often causes pain, but in rare cases, it can lead to intraperitoneal hemorrhage. In this case, a preoperative diagnosis was possible, but the accessory spleen did not enhance due to torsion, and its broad attachment to the hematoma made the boundary unclear, making it difficult to diagnose torsion on contrast-enhanced CT. On the other hand, MRI revealed different signal intensities between the hematoma and the accessory spleen, making it easier to identify accessory spleen. Additionally, in this case, clear delayed enhancement was observed on contrast-enhanced T1-weighted imaging, which was useful for diagnosis. It was thought that venous occlusion due to torsion caused the delayed enhancement.In cases of perisplenic hematoma in young individuals, differential diagnoses include SAM, splenic injury, and spontaneous splenic rupture. In addition, bleeding from an accessory spleen must also be considered. A torsioned accessory spleen can be difficult to detect on CT, especially when it is broadly attached to a hematoma. In such cases, the presence of vessels in the pedicle is crucial for diagnosis. MRI is also useful for diagnosis because the signal intensities of the hematoma and the accessory spleen are different.

Patient consent

Written, informed consent was obtained from the patient(s) for the publication of their case details. The patient(s) have reviewed the case report and understand that their medical information will be published. All necessary measures were taken to ensure the patient(s)' anonymity, and identifying details have been removed or anonymized to protect their privacy.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** Male, in his 20s.
- **Chief Complaint:** Acute left upper abdominal pain.
- **History of Present Illness:** Persistent tightness in the left upper abdomen since the evening prior, worsening to severe pain by late night, aggravated by deep inhalation and the supine position.
- **Past Medical History:** None reported.
- **Personal History:** None reported.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Mild tachycardia (heart rate: 93 bpm), left upper quadrant pain, no tenderness or abdominal distention, no palpable masses.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Blood tests: Mildly elevated WBC (9,800/µl) and CRP (2.11 mg/dl). Hemoglobin normal at 16.2 g/dl with no coagulation abnormalities.
  - Imaging:
    - Non-contrast CT: High attenuation around the spleen suggestive of hematoma.
    - Contrast-enhanced CT: Homogeneous splenic enhancement; 25-mm round nodule anterior to the spleen with no contrast enhancement, suspected as an accessory spleen with hematoma.
    - Angiography: Tapered occlusion of artery to the accessory spleen with no extravasation of contrast.
    - MRI: Differences in signal intensity between the accessory spleen and hematoma; delayed enhancement of the accessory spleen due to venous congestion.
  - Surgical findings: Accessory spleen slightly paler than usual; hemorrhage and congestion noted.
  - Pathology: Evidence of bleeding from the pedicle and mild congestion; no infarction.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:** Based on acute left upper quadrant pain in a young adult, possible causes such as splenic injury, spontaneous splenic rupture, and arterial rupture secondary to SAM were considered.
2. **Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
   - **Spontaneous splenic rupture or injury:** Ruling out based on the absence of trauma, normal splenic morphology on imaging, and no diffuse splenic injury.
   - **Segmental arterial mediolysis (SAM):** Excluded due to lack of abnormalities in surrounding arteries on angiography.
   - **Inflammation or hematologic causes:** Absence of significant WBC elevation or splenic enlargement made these less likely.
3. **Accessory Spleen Torsion with Hemorrhage:** This diagnosis remained plausible given the findings of a dead/poorly enhancing accessory spleen on imaging, the hematoma, and angiographic evidence of vascular occlusion.

Rationale for Confirming Accessory Spleen Torsion:
- The accessory spleen was broad-based and poorly contrasted in CT but better delineated on MRI due to differences in signal intensity between it and the hematoma.
- Gradual enhancement on delayed-phase imaging suggested venous congestion due to torsion rather than complete infarction or arterial occlusion.
- Pathological confirmation of hemorrhage and congestion in the accessory spleen with bleeding from the pedicle strongly supported this diagnosis.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Intraperitoneal hemorrhage due to torsion of an accessory spleen.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Intraperitoneal hemorrhage due to torsion of an accessory spleen.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient presents with acute left upper abdominal pain, onsetting the previous evening and worsening significantly overnight. The pain aggravation by deep inhalation and supine position is suggestive of irritation to the diaphragm and positional influence on the affected organ in the left upper quadrant. The acute and severe nature points towards an acute or subacute process.

<step 2> **Evaluate Physical Examination Findings:** Mild tachycardia indicates a physiological response to pain or stress. Localized pain in the left upper quadrant aligns with the patient's complaint. The absence of tenderness, abdominal distention, and palpable masses is somewhat non-specific but helps to rule out certain acute abdominal conditions that usually present with these signs.

<step 3> **Interpret Blood Test Results:** Mildly elevated WBC and CRP are indicators of inflammation, which is non-specific but supports an acute inflammatory or irritative process. Normal hemoglobin and coagulation profile rule out significant blood loss or coagulopathy as primary concerns.

<step 4> **Analyze Imaging Findings (CT, Angiography, MRI):**
    - **Non-contrast CT:** High attenuation around the spleen strongly suggests hematoma (blood accumulation).
    - **Contrast-enhanced CT:** Confirms hematoma and identifies a 25mm non-enhancing round nodule anterior to the spleen, initially suspected as an accessory spleen with hematoma. Homogeneous enhancement of the main spleen indicates normal perfusion.
    - **Angiography:** Tapered occlusion of the artery to the accessory spleen with no extravasation suggests a vascular compromise to the accessory spleen, likely causing the hematoma. The absence of extravasation rules out active bleeding at the time of angiography.
    - **MRI:** Confirms differences in signal intensity between the accessory spleen and hematoma and reveals delayed enhancement of the accessory spleen, highly suggestive of venous congestion.

<step 5> **Review Surgical and Pathology Findings:**
    - **Surgical Findings:** A paler accessory spleen with hemorrhage and congestion directly supports the imaging findings and confirms a pathological process within the accessory spleen.
    - **Pathology:** Evidence of bleeding from the pedicle and mild congestion, without infarction, is crucial. This indicates that the primary issue is venous outflow obstruction and hemorrhage rather than arterial blockage leading to tissue death (infarction).

<step 6> **Synthesize all findings and formulate a diagnosis:**  The constellation of acute left upper quadrant pain, hematoma around a non-enhancing nodule (accessory spleen), angiography showing tapered arterial occlusion and MRI showing delayed enhancement (venous congestion), along with surgical and pathological confirmation of hemorrhage and congestion (without infarction) in an accessory spleen strongly points towards **torsion of the accessory spleen pedicle.** The torsion would lead to venous outflow obstruction, causing congestion and hemorrhage, and potentially secondary arterial compression explaining the tapered occlusion.

<step 7> **Consider Differential Diagnoses and Rule them out:** Based on the specific imaging and pathological findings, other common causes of acute abdominal pain like splenic rupture (less likely without trauma history and more diffuse hemorrhage expected), splenic infarction (pathology ruled out infarction), splenic abscess (imaging and clinical picture less suggestive), pancreatitis (imaging not suggestive), and other general abdominal pain causes are less likely compared to the focused findings related to the accessory spleen.

### Answer:
Accessory spleen torsion with hemorrhage

reasoning_eval.step: **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient presents with acute left upper abdominal pain, onsetting the previous evening and worsening significantly overnight. The pain aggravation by deep inhalation and supine position is suggestive of irritation to the diaphragm and positional influence on the affected organ in the left upper quadrant. The acute and severe nature points towards an acute or subacute process.

**Evaluate Physical Examination Findings:** Mild tachycardia indicates a physiological response to pain or stress. Localized pain in the left upper quadrant aligns with the patient's complaint. The absence of tenderness, abdominal distention, and palpable masses is somewhat non-specific but helps to rule out certain acute abdominal conditions that usually present with these signs.

**Interpret Blood Test Results:** Mildly elevated WBC and CRP are indicators of inflammation, which is non-specific but supports an acute inflammatory or irritative process. Normal hemoglobin and coagulation profile rule out significant blood loss or coagulopathy as primary concerns.

**Analyze Imaging Findings (CT, Angiography, MRI):**
    - **Non-contrast CT:** High attenuation around the spleen strongly suggests hematoma (blood accumulation).
    - **Contrast-enhanced CT:** Confirms hematoma and identifies a 25mm non-enhancing round nodule anterior to the spleen, initially suspected as an accessory spleen with hematoma. Homogeneous enhancement of the main spleen indicates normal perfusion.
    - **Angiography:** Tapered occlusion of the artery to the accessory spleen with no extravasation suggests a vascular compromise to the accessory spleen, likely causing the hematoma. The absence of extravasation rules out active bleeding at the time of angiography.
    - **MRI:** Confirms differences in signal intensity between the accessory spleen and hematoma and reveals delayed enhancement of the accessory spleen, highly suggestive of venous congestion.

**Review Surgical and Pathology Findings:**
    - **Surgical Findings:** A paler accessory spleen with hemorrhage and congestion directly supports the imaging findings and confirms a pathological process within the accessory spleen.
    - **Pathology:** Evidence of bleeding from the pedicle and mild congestion, without infarction, is crucial. This indicates that the primary issue is venous outflow obstruction and hemorrhage rather than arterial blockage leading to tissue death (infarction).

**Synthesize all findings and formulate a diagnosis:**  The constellation of acute left upper quadrant pain, hematoma around a non-enhancing nodule (accessory spleen), angiography showing tapered arterial occlusion and MRI showing delayed enhancement (venous congestion), along with surgical and pathological confirmation of hemorrhage and congestion (without infarction) in an accessory spleen strongly points towards **torsion of the accessory spleen pedicle.** The torsion would lead to venous outflow obstruction, causing congestion and hemorrhage, and potentially secondary arterial compression explaining the tapered occlusion.

**Consider Differential Diagnoses and Rule them out:** Based on the specific imaging and pathological findings, other common causes of acute abdominal pain like splenic rupture (less likely without trauma history and more diffuse hemorrhage expected), splenic infarction (pathology ruled out infarction), splenic abscess (imaging and clinical picture less suggestive), pancreatitis (imaging not suggestive), and other general abdominal pain causes are less likely compared to the focused findings related to the accessory spleen.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Acute left upper quadrant pain in a young adult suggests possible causes including splenic injury, spontaneous splenic rupture, arterial rupture secondary to SAM, inflammation, or hematologic causes.
<Step 2> Spontaneous splenic rupture or injury ruled out due to absence of trauma, normal splenic morphology on imaging, and no diffuse splenic injury.
<Step 3> Segmental arterial mediolysis (SAM) excluded due to lack of abnormalities in surrounding arteries on angiography.
<Step 4> Inflammation or hematologic causes deemed less likely due to absence of significant WBC elevation or splenic enlargement.

<Step 5> Accessory spleen torsion with hemorrhage remained a plausible diagnosis given the findings of a dead/poorly enhancing accessory spleen on imaging, the presence of a hematoma, and angiographic evidence of vascular occlusion.
<Step 6> Accessory spleen appeared broad-based and poorly contrasted on CT but better delineated on MRI due to differences in signal intensity between it and the hematoma.
<Step 7> Gradual enhancement on delayed-phase imaging indicated venous congestion due to torsion rather than complete infarction or arterial occlusion.
<Step 8> Pathological confirmation of hemorrhage and congestion in the accessory spleen with bleeding from the pedicle strongly supported the diagnosis of accessory spleen torsion.
<Step 9> Final diagnosis confirmed as intraperitoneal hemorrhage due to torsion of an accessory spleen.
